name: Commonwealth Catholic Charities
description_short: Catholic nonprofit providing free housing counseling, financial education, and credit improvement assistance across Virginia, plus comprehensive support for families, immigrants, and vulnerable populations.
description_long: Commonwealth Catholic Charities (CCC) is a Virginia-based nonprofit organization serving multiple communities across the state including Richmond, Roanoke, Hopewell, Newport News, and Norton. The organization focuses on three core mission areas: nurturing children and families, welcoming new Americans, and housing the most vulnerable populations. Operating since at least the early 2000s based on their service maturity, CCC has established itself as a comprehensive social services provider with deep community roots. CCC offers Housing & Financial Counseling explicitly listed on their services menu, available in Richmond and Hopewell locations. Their financial counseling services include credit score improvement, budget creation, home purchase preparation, and foreclosure avoidance—positioning them squarely within the free nonprofit credit and financial counseling space. These services appear integrated with broader supportive housing and case management offerings rather than as standalone products. The organization distinguishes itself through its integrated approach combining credit/financial counseling with immediate housing support, street outreach, refugee resettlement, and immigration legal services. Rather than focusing narrowly on credit repair, CCC positions financial counseling as one component of holistic family stabilization. Their team includes trained interpreters and immigration attorneys, indicating sophisticated capacity to serve diverse populations. Many services are explicitly free or subsidized, targeting low-income and vulnerable populations rather than creditworthy consumers. CCC represents the traditional nonprofit social services model: comprehensive, mission-driven, and funded through donations and grants rather than consumer fees. The primary limitation is geographic—services concentrate in five Virginia locations and may not serve surrounding areas. Additionally, while they offer financial counseling, this appears less specialized than dedicated credit counseling nonprofits; financial education is bundled with housing and family services rather than being their primary focus. Consumers seeking intensive credit dispute services or specialized credit builder products would be better served by dedicated credit-focused nonprofits.
pros: Free housing and financial counseling (credit improvement, budgeting, homebuying, foreclosure prevention) with no apparent fees
HUD-implied capability through housing counseling services, suggesting quality standards and legitimate nonprofit credentials
Integrated services combining financial counseling with immediate housing support, street outreach, and case management for stabilization
On-site interpreters and translation services available across multiple languages for immigrant families
Immigration legal services offering affordable consultations alongside financial services for new Americans
Multiple locations across Virginia (Richmond, Roanoke, Hopewell, Newport News, Norton) with specialized local programs
Transparent service directory clearly listing what's available, by location, with contact methods (804-285-5900, agency@cccofva.org)
cons: Geographic limitation: Services only available in five Virginia cities; no remote/online counseling mentioned for surrounding areas
Financial counseling appears secondary to broader family/housing services rather than a specialized credit focus
No specific mention of credit monitoring tools, credit reports review, or dispute processes—services described as educational rather than remedial
Limited detail on financial counseling curriculum or outcomes; website emphasizes adoption, foster care, and refugee services more prominently
No pricing transparency or financial aid application process described; unclear how services are prioritized or allocated when demand exceeds capacity
best_for: Low-income Virginia residents (Richmond, Roanoke, Hopewell, Newport News, Norton) needing budget help and credit improvement advice alongside housing support
Immigrant and refugee families needing financial counseling plus immigration legal services and language support
Individuals experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity who need integrated case management with financial education
Families facing foreclosure who need counseling and housing stability services from a trusted nonprofit
diagnosis: Commonwealth Catholic Charities is best for low-income or vulnerable Virginians in their five service areas who need financial counseling integrated with housing stability, family support, or immigration services. Main caveat: This is a comprehensive social services nonprofit, not a specialized credit counseling agency—their credit/financial focus is secondary to broader family and housing stabilization missions, and services are geographically limited to five Virginia cities.
services: Housing & Financial Counseling (credit improvement, budgeting, home purchase preparation, foreclosure avoidance)
Housing Resource Center (day shelter, phone charging, food, clothing, toiletries)
Supportive Housing with subsidized rent and case management
Interpreter & Translation Services (health care, legal, social, school, business contexts)
Workforce Development (job training, placement, interview prep)
Youth Hub for ages 18-24 experiencing homelessness
typical_results_timeline: Counseling available within 1-2 weeks of contact.
address: 2131 Valley View Blvd Nw, Roanoke, VA 24012
